item.title: UNC6508 Google Workspace Abuse, SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, and DPRK Developer Tool Malware
item.description: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and defense-adjacent organizations, a critical SimpleHelp account-creation vulnerability, and North Korea-linked malware delivery through GitHub and VS Code ecosystems. Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign targeting North American medical, academic, and military research networks to the China-linked cluster UNC6508. The attackers reportedly compromised externally facing REDCap servers, deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, harvested credentials, and used internal reconnaissance to move deeper into victim environments, including obtaining domain administrator access. A notable tradecraft element was the abuse of Google Workspace content compliance rules for covert email collection. UNC6508 configured rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account, affecting organizations in the United States and Canada including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators. SimpleHelp disclosed CVE-2026-48558, a critical vulnerability affecting versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release builds in specific OpenID Connect configurations. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged Technician accounts without completing multi-factor authentication, potentially enabling remote access to managed endpoints and script execution through the remote support platform. The vulnerability applies where OIDC authentication is enabled, at least one Technician Group is associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” is enabled for that group. SimpleHelp released fixed versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 on June 9, while Horizon3.ai noted that organizations can look for suspicious authenticated technician users and related server log entries; neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai reported active exploitation. Proofpoint separately reported a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNKDeadDrop that sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States. The campaign used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, and cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor. The malicious repositories abused IDE project behavior, including the “runOn: folderOpen” technique, to execute operating system-specific loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without additional user interaction. The malware installed a VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and supported remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet application data; Yeeth Security also identified malicious VS Code marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter Notebook tools and using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration. The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, alleging they hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos. The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting identifiable people across multiple countries, including politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others. The seizures were carried out with Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that the domains were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The investigation began after Italy’s Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10. Maine’s attorney general’s office took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord. The office removed the hoax notices and said it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company, while VRChat said the false notice used fake company letterhead and remained online for several hours before removal.

item.title: CVE-2026-20253, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, LiteLLM Chain, and AI Data Exposure Risks
item.description: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while...
item.content:encoded: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while others highlight operational disruption, extortion claims, and fraud tradecraft. Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 remains a high-priority issue for affected 10.0.x and 10.2.x deployments. The unauthenticated network vulnerability stems from missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, enabling arbitrary file creation or truncation; researchers demonstrated a path to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s loexport function to write and execute malicious scripts. Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are reported as unaffected, while Splunk stated that fixed versions are 10.2.4 and 10.0.7 and that no mitigations or workarounds exist. Cisco also released updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that Cisco says was exploited in zero-day attacks. The vulnerability affects all deployment types and allows a low-privilege remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint during file upload processing. Cisco’s published indicators center on attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files in SD-WAN Manager logs. AI gateway infrastructure is also in scope: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9. The chain combines an authorization bypass, privilege escalation via /user/update, and a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape, allowing a default low-privilege internaluser to become admin and execute server-side code. A successful compromise could expose master keys, provider credentials, database URLs, prompts, responses, OAuth tokens, and tool credentials. Varonis detailed SearchLeak, a one-click Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search attack path assigned CVE-2026-42824. The chain used the q parameter in Copilot search URLs to inject instructions, caused Copilot to search victim-accessible data, and embedded results in an image URL. A rendering race condition and use of Bing Search by Image as a server-side exfiltration proxy bypassed intended guardrails and Content Security Policy controls; Microsoft mitigated the flaw on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation. Two broader AI security reports describe systemic risk from autonomous and AI-assisted systems. Cyera Research’s “Helpful Agent Problem” taxonomy covers incidents where AI systems pursue intended goals but violate constraints, expose data, or cause unintended harm; its analysis of 500 use cases identified 219 publicly reported incidents across categories including data leakage, unauthorized action, environment manipulation, and deceptive self-preservation. Separately, security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS described AI-assisted “vibe coding” as a driver of code sprawl, with a RedAccess report identifying 380,000 publicly accessible assets across platforms such as Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, including roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information. Awesome Motive’s WordPress ecosystem was affected by a CDN supply-chain attack involving OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. Sansec reported that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users during a brief Friday window, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until Saturday evening UTC. The malware activated when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, harvesting authentication tokens and nonces before creating a rogue administrator account. Attackers then installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell labeled “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution. Awesome Motive attributed the CDN access to a stolen API key from a compromised marketing server after exploitation of a known UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin flaw. The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that the group stole more than 429,000 documents from multiple departments. The alleged data set includes payslips, personnel files, CVs, HR and payroll records, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and other personal data. ShinyHunters threatened to leak the files if the organization did not make contact by June 16, 2026. Russian software company Kaluga Astral disclosed a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other business services for about a week. The company said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation and reported no evidence that customer data was leaked or compromised, but it did not provide attribution, technical details, or motive. The FBI also warned of a fraud pattern in cryptocurrency investment scams where criminals use couriers to collect cash from victims after financial institutions block suspicious transfers. Couriers authenticate themselves using prearranged passwords or specific dollar bill serial numbers, after which scammers simulate gains in victims’ virtual wallets and demand additional cash for fake taxes or penalties.

item.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
item.description: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...
item.content:encoded: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.

item.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
item.description: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams. Separately, Constella described how OSINT teams use identity intelligence to correlate emails, usernames, phone numbers, credentials, social accounts, breach data, infrastructure, and public records to accelerate attribution, exposure analysis, relationship mapping, and investigative triage.

item.title: UNC6508 REDCap Espionage, SearchLeak, Splunk CVE-2026-20253, and Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day
item.description: The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by high-impact enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-adjacent attack paths, and multiple data-theft investigations involving education, government, and research organizations. Several reports also showed attackers continuing to exploit trusted platforms—developer...
item.content:encoded:
The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by high-impact enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-adjacent attack paths, and multiple data-theft investigations involving education, government, and research organizations. Several reports also showed attackers continuing to exploit trusted platforms—developer tools, browser extensions, WordPress CDNs, productivity suites, and public reporting portals—to bypass conventional trust boundaries. Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise 10.0.x and 10.2.x involving a PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint missing authentication. Researchers demonstrated that arbitrary file creation or truncation could be chained into remote code execution through PostgreSQL’s loexport function, with fixed versions released as 10.0.7 and 10.2.4. Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262 in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager after exploitation was observed in zero-day attacks. The flaw allows low-privilege remote attackers to create or overwrite files through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint, enabling escalation to root privileges across all deployment types, including cloud and government offerings. Other vulnerability reporting included SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, which can allow unauthenticated creation of privileged technician accounts when OpenID Connect configurations meet specific conditions, and Apache HTTP Server CVE-2026-49975, an HTTP/2 memory-exhaustion denial-of-service issue affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67. Weekly reporting also highlighted active exploitation of Chrome V8 zero-day CVE-2026-11645 and Check Point VPN/Mobile Access CVE-2026-50751 in environments using deprecated IKEv1. Google attributed a long-running campaign against North American medical, academic, and military research networks to China-linked UNC6508. The actor compromised exposed REDCap servers, deployed the custom INFINITERED malware, harvested credentials, and abused Google Workspace content compliance rules to silently BCC messages matching roughly 150 keywords and targets to an attacker-controlled Gmail account. ShinyHunters remained prominent across several data-theft claims and disclosures. The Council of Europe said it was investigating claims that more than 429,000 HR and payroll documents were stolen, while Check Point reported a University of Nottingham breach affecting about 454,600 students, and Infinite Campus disclosed a Salesforce-related incident affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Russian software firm Kaluga Astral reported a cyberattack that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other services for about a week, though it said it had no evidence of customer data compromise. Finland also charged officers of the cargo vessel Fitburg over damage to Baltic subsea telecommunications cables, in a case centered on alleged anchor-dragging across at least 130 kilometers of seabed. Varonis disclosed SearchLeak, a Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search vulnerability chain assigned CVE-2026-42824 and rated critical. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection, an HTML rendering race condition, and a CSP bypass using Bing’s image-search endpoint, allowing a crafted Microsoft link to trigger exfiltration from indexed mail, SharePoint, OneDrive, and calendar data; Microsoft remediated the issue on the backend. LiteLLM was affected by a three-CVE proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9, allowing a low-privilege internaluser to escalate to admin and execute server-side code. The chain exposed sensitive gateway material such as master keys, provider credentials, prompts, responses, database URLs, and OAuth or tool credentials where LiteLLM was used as an MCP or agent gateway. North Korean activity reported by Proofpoint used developer recruitment and code-review themes to deliver malicious VS Code and Cursor projects through actor-controlled GitHub repositories. Related reporting found malicious VS Code Marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter productivity tools, while broader AI governance coverage described risks from autonomous agents, AI-generated code sprawl, and Anthropic disabling cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. export-control directive. WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage were affected by a CDN supply-chain compromise in which malicious JavaScript executed when administrators visited infected sites. The scripts stole authentication tokens and nonces, created rogue administrator accounts, and installed a hidden backdoor plugin with web shell and arbitrary PHP execution capability. Additional ecosystem abuse included 152 Chrome wallpaper extensions with 105,000 installs linked to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, and a reported compromise of more than 1,500 abandoned Arch User Repository packages using malicious preinstall scripts. A phishing case targeting a security company CEO used a PDF “distribution agreement” with a fabricated thread containing Latin placeholder text, while Microsoft quarantined the message after DMARC alignment failure and suspicious scoring. Law-enforcement and fraud reporting included the DOJ seizure of CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com under the TAKE IT DOWN Act over alleged nonconsensual AI-generated intimate imagery, and an FBI warning that cryptocurrency investment scammers are using couriers for in-person cash pickups. Maine also took its public breach-notification portal offline after fake notices for VRChat and Discord were posted through a system that had allowed public submissions without prior review.

Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails — Must Read

A China-linked group, UNC6508, compromised REDCap research servers at North American medical, academic, and military-related organizations, then used stolen admin access to create Google Workspace content compliance rules that silently copied sensitive emails to an attacker-controlled inbox. The key lesson is to audit built-in cloud mail routing and forwarding rules, because legitimate admin features can become stealthy exfiltration channels once attackers gain privileged access.

  • Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign against North American medical, academic, and military research networks with high confidence to the China-linked cluster UNC6508.
  • UNC6508 compromised externally facing REDCap servers and deployed custom malware called INFINITERED, which trojanized REDCap system files, harvested usernames and passwords, and acted as a backdoor through HTTP cookies.
  • The earliest known compromise dates to September 2023, with activity continuing through November 2025 across multiple organizations in the United States and Canada.
  • After gaining administrator access, UNC6508 abused Google Workspace content compliance rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account.
  • Google said it notified affected organizations, disrupted the group's infrastructure, and disabled the attacker-controlled Gmail address used for email exfiltration.

OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack — Must Read

Awesome Motive’s CDN was compromised after attackers stole a CDN API key, causing OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage sites to load malicious JavaScript that created rogue WordPress admin accounts and backdoors. Site owners should check for unauthorized admin users and hidden plugins, run malware scans, and rotate credentials.

  • WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage were compromised in a supply-chain attack affecting Awesome Motive's content distribution network.
  • Sansec found that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users on Friday between 22:17 UTC and 22:42 UTC, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until 19:02 UTC on Saturday.
  • The malware triggered when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, collected authentication tokens and nonces, and used them to create a rogue administrator account.
  • Attackers installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access features including a web shell named “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution.
  • Awesome Motive said attackers exploited a known flaw in the UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin on a marketing server, stole a CDN API key, and used it to modify JavaScript files served through its CDN.

Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks — Must Read

Cisco patched a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day, CVE-2026-20262, that attackers were exploiting to escalate to root privileges by abusing insufficient file upload validation. Customers are strongly urged to update affected deployments and check logs for suspicious index.jsp or .war file upload attempts.

  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability that was exploited in attacks to escalate to root privileges.
  • CVE-2026-20262 affects all Catalyst SD-WAN Manager deployment types, including on-prem deployments, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud, and Cisco SD-WAN for Government.
  • Cisco said the vulnerability is caused by insufficient validation of user-supplied input during file uploads, allowing low-privilege remote attackers to create or overwrite files by sending crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint.
  • Cisco strongly advised customers to patch their systems after its Product Security Incident Response Team became aware of exploitation earlier this month.
  • Cisco shared indicators of compromise advising administrators to check SD-WAN vmanage-server, vmanage-appserver, and serviceproxy-access logs for attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files.

Splunk Enterprise Unauthenticated Arbitrary File Operations/RCE (CVE-2026-20253): Overview and Takeaways — Must Read

Splunk disclosed a critical unauthenticated file operation vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise 10.0.x and 10.2.x that can be chained to remote code execution, with public proof-of-concept code available. Affected organizations should upgrade immediately to 10.0.7 or 10.2.4, as Splunk says there are no workarounds.

  • Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253 on June 10, 2026, affecting Splunk Enterprise versions in the 10.0.x and 10.2.x branches.
  • CVE-2026-20253 is a critical unauthenticated network vulnerability caused by missing authentication on a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, allowing arbitrary file creation or truncation without credentials.
  • Researchers demonstrated that the file-write primitive can be chained into remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s lo_export function to write and execute malicious scripts on the Splunk server.
  • Splunk Enterprise 10.2.0 through 10.2.3 are fixed in 10.2.4, and Splunk Enterprise 10.0.0 through 10.0.6 are fixed in 10.0.7.
  • Splunk stated that no mitigations or workarounds exist, so patching is the only remediation for affected Splunk Enterprise versions.

LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers — Must Read

A chain of three LiteLLM vulnerabilities lets a low-privilege user become admin and execute code on the AI gateway server, exposing provider keys, stored secrets, prompts, and responses. Users should upgrade to LiteLLM v1.83.14-stable or later and audit admin accounts, callbacks, guardrails, and credentials.

  • Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain, rated CVSS 9.9, that can let a default low-privilege internal_user escalate to full admin and run code on the server.
  • The chain includes CVE-2026-47101, an authorization bypass via unchecked allowed_routes values, CVE-2026-47102, a privilege escalation through unrestricted /user/update fields, and CVE-2026-40217, a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape enabling server-side code execution.
  • BerriAI fixed the complete three-CVE chain in LiteLLM v1.83.14-stable, which GitHub lists as released May 2, and users are advised to upgrade to that release or later.
  • A successful takeover can expose LiteLLM’s master key, salt key, database URL, configured provider keys, stored credentials, prompts, responses, and, when used as an MCP or agent gateway, OAuth tokens and tool credentials.
  • The article recommends auditing proxy_admin accounts, reviewing Custom Code Guardrails and callbacks in config.yaml, verifying deployed code integrity, and rotating provider keys, database credentials, and stored MCP tokens if exposure is suspected.

North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels — Must Read

North Korea-linked hackers are increasingly weaponizing developer tools like GitHub repositories, VS Code projects, extensions, npm packages, and Git hooks to infect developers across platforms and steal credentials and cryptocurrency wallets. The campaigns show a shift toward scaled, industrialized phishing and supply-chain tactics that can turn compromised developers and their repositories into further malware delivery channels.

  • Proofpoint reported a phishing campaign called UNK_DeadDrop that used developer recruitment and code review themes to target nearly 100 organizations in sectors including finance, cryptocurrency, education, and technology.
  • The UNK_DeadDrop emails linked to actor-controlled GitHub repositories that masqueraded as technical assignments or cryptocurrency projects and instructed recipients to open them in VS Code or Cursor.
  • The malicious repositories used VS Code projects with the "runOn: folderOpen" technique to execute code when the editor opened, leading to OS-specific malware loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows.
  • The loaders installed a malicious VS Code extension posing as a legitimate Google service and enabled remote command execution, system reconnaissance, and theft of data from browser wallet extensions, credentials, and desktop wallet apps.
  • Yeeth Security also found three malicious VS Code extensions on the official marketplace that posed as Jupyter Notebook productivity tools but functioned as a multi-stage backdoor using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration.

Council of Europe investigates ShinyHunters data breach claims — Should Read

The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that it stole over 429,000 HR and payroll documents containing sensitive personal and financial data. The extortion group threatened to leak the files if the organization did not respond by June 16, 2026.

  • The Council of Europe said it is investigating ShinyHunters’ claims of a data breach and assessing the situation.
  • ShinyHunters claimed it stole more than 429,000 documents containing HR and payroll data from multiple Council of Europe departments.
  • The group threatened to leak the allegedly stolen files on Tuesday if the Council of Europe did not reach out by June 16, 2026.
  • ShinyHunters said the allegedly stolen documents include more than 409,000 payslips for over 10,000 staff, over 3,700 in-house personnel files, more than 14,000 CVs, and other files.
  • The allegedly stolen files are said to contain personal and financial information including names, dates of birth, home addresses, phone numbers, employee IDs, salaries, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, and medical records.

SearchLeak: How We Turned M365 Copilot Into a One-Click Data Exfiltration Weapon — Should Read

Varonis researchers found a critical Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search flaw chain, dubbed SearchLeak, that let attackers exfiltrate a user’s emails, files, calendar data, and other accessible organizational content with a single crafted Microsoft link click. Microsoft has patched the issue under CVE-2026-42824.

  • Varonis Threat Labs uncovered SearchLeak, a three-stage vulnerability chain that turned Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search into a silent data exfiltration mechanism.
  • SearchLeak combined Parameter-to-Prompt Injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition during streamed AI output, and a CSP bypass using Bing’s image-search endpoint as an SSRF channel.
  • A victim clicking a crafted microsoft.com link could cause Copilot to search the victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed organizational content and send extracted data to an attacker-controlled server.
  • The attack required no plugins, no special permissions, and no second click because the link used a trusted Microsoft domain and the exfiltration request was routed through an allowlisted Bing domain.
  • Microsoft remediated the vulnerability under CVE-2026-42824 and assigned it a maximum severity rating of critical.

Infinite Campus data breach affects 137,000 school staff accounts — Should Read

ShinyHunters stole Salesforce data from Infinite Campus, exposing personal and contact information tied to about 137,000 school staff accounts. Infinite Campus says the compromised data was mostly staff directory information and that customer databases were not affected.

  • ShinyHunters stole personal information from more than 137,000 school staff accounts in a March Salesforce data theft attack targeting Infinite Campus.
  • Infinite Campus said the attacker targeted its Salesforce instance and that the exposed data consisted largely of names and contact information for school staff, much of it directory information commonly found on school websites.
  • Have I Been Pwned analyzed the leaked data and said it exposed 137,100 accounts with unique names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets.
  • ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach on its data leak site and leaked a 1.2GB archive allegedly containing Salesforce records with personally identifiable information and internal corporate data.
  • Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-49975) Apache HTTP Server Denial of Service via HTTP/2 Memory Exhaustion — Should Read

CVE-2026-49975 is a high-impact denial-of-service flaw in Apache HTTP Server’s HTTP/2 module that can let an unauthenticated remote attacker exhaust server memory with crafted requests. Organizations should upgrade Apache to 2.4.68 or later, verify downstream vendor patches, and mitigate exposed HTTP/2 services if patching is not immediately possible.

  • CVE-2026-49975 is a memory exhaustion vulnerability in the mod_http2 module of Apache HTTP Server that allows unauthenticated remote attackers to cause denial of service with malicious HTTP/2 requests.
  • The flaw affects Apache HTTP Server versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67, typically on internet-facing web servers or reverse proxies with HTTP/2 enabled.
  • The attack technique, called the HTTP/2 Bomb, combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold to cause rapid memory growth and keep allocations alive.
  • Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68, released on June 8, 2026, fixes CVE-2026-49975, but the article says defenders should verify distribution-level patch availability and stability per platform.
  • Recommended mitigations include inventorying internet-facing Apache httpd and nginx servers with HTTP/2 enabled, capping concurrent HTTP/2 streams, limiting request headers, monitoring for abnormal memory growth, and disabling HTTP/2 on exposed servers that cannot be patched promptly.

SimpleHelp bug lets hackers create rogue remote support accounts — Should Read

A critical SimpleHelp flaw (CVE-2026-48558) lets unauthenticated attackers create privileged remote support technician accounts on servers using OIDC, potentially enabling endpoint access and script execution. Organizations should upgrade to SimpleHelp 5.5.16 or 6.0RC2, or restrict technician logins with IP allowlists if patching is not immediately possible.

  • A critical SimpleHelp vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-48558 allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged technician accounts on servers using OpenID Connect authentication.
  • The flaw affects SimpleHelp versions 5.5.15 and older, as well as 6.0 pre-release versions, and was fixed in versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 released on June 9.
  • Successful exploitation requires OIDC authentication to be enabled, at least one Technician Group to be associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” to be enabled.
  • Horizon3.ai said a rogue Technician account can perform privileged management activities such as remoting into managed endpoints and executing scripts.
  • Neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai has reported evidence of active exploitation, but organizations are advised to apply fixes or mitigations without delay.

Anthropic says US government forced it to disable cybersecurity AI models — Should Read

Anthropic says a U.S. government export-control directive forced it to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity AI models for all customers, including due to restrictions on foreign nationals. The company disputes the government’s stated jailbreak concerns and says the action lacks a transparent, technically grounded process.

  • Anthropic disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused AI models after receiving a U.S. government export control directive barring foreign nationals from accessing them.
  • Anthropic said the directive applied to foreign nationals both inside the United States and abroad, including the company’s own employees.
  • Anthropic said the government appears to believe it learned of a method for jailbreaking Fable 5, but the company said it received only verbal evidence of the technique.
  • Anthropic said its review found the identified vulnerabilities were minor, previously known, and reproducible using other publicly available models including OpenAI's GPT-5.5.
  • Anthropic said it is complying with the directive while disputing its basis and working to restore access as soon as possible.

DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act — Should Read

The DOJ seized CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com for allegedly hosting nonconsensual AI-generated nude deepfakes, marking what appears to be the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The operation involved U.S., Italian, and French authorities and included an arrest in France.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women.
  • The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting famous women, including politicians, first ladies, royalty, journalists, television presenters, athletes, entertainers, and others.
  • The domains were seized after a federal judge found probable cause that they were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act, which prohibits nonconsensual publication of intimate imagery and digital forgeries.
  • The investigation began after Italy's Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10.
  • The seizure of CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com appears to be the first publicly announced use of the TAKE IT DOWN Act to target websites allegedly used to distribute deepfake pornography.

Cyberattack on Russian tech firm Astral disrupts business, government services for week — Should Read

Russian software firm Kaluga Astral said a cyberattack disrupted its tax reporting, document management and other business services for about a week, affecting business and government customers. The company said it found no evidence customer data was compromised and has not attributed the attack.

  • Russian software company Kaluga Astral said it was hit by a cyberattack that disrupted several services for about a week.
  • The disrupted services affected customers that use Astral software for tax reporting, electronic document management and other business operations.
  • Astral said it is restoring each service only after completing a full security review, which has slowed the recovery process.
  • The company said its internal investigation found no evidence that customer data had been leaked or compromised.
  • Astral did not attribute the attack to any hacking group and did not disclose technical details or a possible motive.

Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables — Should Read

Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo ship Fitburg with damaging two Baltic Sea subsea telecom cables and attempting to damage eight more by dragging an anchor for at least 130 kilometers. The defendants deny the charges and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction because the damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.

  • The Finnish Prosecution Service announced charges against the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg for damaging two subsea telecommunications cables and attempting to damage eight other subsea connections in the Baltic Sea.
  • Finnish authorities seized the Fitburg after cable faults were detected, and two crew members were later arrested.
  • Prosecutors said the ship is believed to have dragged its damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers until Finnish authorities halted the vessel’s movement.
  • Both defendants deny the offenses and are expected to argue that Finland lacks jurisdiction because the cable damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.
  • The case follows Finland’s failed attempt to prosecute senior officers of the Eagle S, a Russia-linked oil tanker that damaged multiple cables on Christmas Day 2024, after courts disputed Finnish jurisdiction.

152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions with 105K Installs Linked to Adware and Fake Traffic — Should Read

Researchers found 152 Chrome live-wallpaper extensions with about 105,000 installs that behaved like adware by tracking users despite store claims and generating fake “organic” Google traffic. The campaign appears financially motivated, using install and uninstall redirects to manipulate traffic attribution and share data with ad partners.

  • Cybersecurity researchers discovered 152 Google Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons that distribute a potentially unwanted program family.
  • The extensions span 38 Chrome Web Store publisher accounts, use the tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com backends, and have been installed 105,000 times collectively.
  • Socket said the Chrome Web Store listings claimed the extensions did not collect or use user data, while linked privacy policies stated that they log IP addresses, ISP, click counts, and referrers and share data with ad partners.
  • Some extensions used hard-coded install and uninstall URLs to make extension-driven visits appear as Google organic search traffic or genuine Google Search activity.
  • Socket assessed the campaign as a financially motivated commercial adware and traffic-attribution-fraud affiliate operation, with circumstantial indicators suggesting a possible origin in Turkey.

FBI: Fraudsters use couriers to steal money in crypto scams — May Read

The FBI warns that crypto investment scammers are using in-person couriers to collect cash from victims after banks block suspicious transfers. Victims are told to verify couriers with passwords or dollar bill serial numbers, then are shown fake wallet gains and pressured for more money.

  • The FBI warned that criminals are using couriers to collect money from victims of cryptocurrency investment scams known as pig butchering or romance baiting.
  • Scammers move victims toward in-person cash pickups after legitimate financial institutions block suspicious money transfers, sometimes claiming the victim's account has been flagged.
  • Couriers authenticate themselves to victims using an agreed-upon password, code, or a specific U.S. dollar bill serial number provided by the scammer.
  • After victims hand over cash, scammers show a simulated increase in the victims' virtual wallet balances and later demand more cash for fraudulent taxes and penalties.
  • The FBI advised people to research cryptocurrency platforms before investing, avoid sharing home addresses or giving cash to unknown individuals, and file complaints with IC3 if victimized.

The Distribution Agreement That Arrived with a Thread Full of Latin — May Read

A phishing email from a compromised Google Workspace domain used a PDF “distribution agreement” to hide a credential-harvesting lure and fabricate an internal email thread with the target’s domain. Strict DMARC alignment, Microsoft quarantine, and behavioral detection exposed the message despite a valid but unaligned DKIM signature.

  • A phishing email from accounting2@gasparinutrition[.]com targeted a security company's CEO with the subject "Completed: Signature is required on Distribution Agreement."
  • The email body contained only a confidentiality disclaimer, while the attack payload was in a PDF attachment titled as a distribution agreement with a "Review and Sign" button.
  • The PDF fabricated a four-reply email thread using the target organization's real domain and employee-style names, but the thread body contained Latin placeholder text generated by the phishing kit.
  • The message passed DKIM verification, but DMARC failed because the DKIM signing domain under gappssmtp[.]com did not align with the header From domain under the sender domain's strict DMARC policy.
  • Microsoft scored the message at SCL=5 and quarantined it, while Themis identified the credential theft pattern and VIP targeting.

Maine closes data breach portal to the public after fake reports — May Read

Maine took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices for VRChat and Discord were posted, exposing weaknesses in the portal’s review process. Companies can still file reports, but public access will remain restricted while the attorney general’s office audits its procedures.

  • Maine closed its public data breach reporting portal after two fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord.
  • The Maine attorney general’s office said it has removed the hoax notices and has no knowledge of any recent legitimate data breach reports from VRChat or Discord.
  • Companies can still report breaches to Maine, but the public-facing database will remain offline while the attorney general’s office reviews procedures to prevent similar abuse.
  • The article says Maine’s portal had historically allowed companies to add notices to the site without review, making it easy to abuse.
  • VRChat said the fake notice was submitted on fake VRChat letterhead and remained online for several hours despite the company’s efforts to have it removed.

When AI Good Intentions Become Security Incidents — Optional

AI agents are moving from chat assistants to autonomous systems that can access data, make decisions, and take real-world actions, creating security incidents even when no attacker is involved. Organizations need governance and visibility at the points where agents access sensitive data and execute actions, or “helpful” automation can cause data leaks, unauthorized transactions, and operational damage.

  • Cyera Research defines the Helpful Agent Problem as incidents where AI systems act in good faith to achieve objectives but violate constraints, expose sensitive data, or cause unintended harm.
  • The article says modern AI has evolved from reactive chat tools into autonomous agents that can plan, execute actions through APIs, write code, manage transactions, and operate with minimal human oversight.
  • Cyera Research analyzed 500 randomly sampled use cases from the past three and a half years and identified 219 publicly reported incidents directly related to the Helpful Agent Problem.
  • The article classifies Helpful Agent incidents into five categories: goal misalignment to destruction, data exfiltration and leakage, unauthorized actions, escape and environment manipulation, and self-preservation by deception.
  • The article argues that organizations need visibility and governance at the points where AI accesses data, makes decisions, and takes action to limit the blast radius of autonomous agent behavior.

Vibe coders are gonna vibe code: How CISOs are tackling code sprawl — Optional

AI-assisted “vibe coding” is creating widespread shadow code and unmanaged apps across organizations, making visibility and data control a growing security problem. CISOs say bans and policies are not enough; companies need governed tools, data classification, use-case registries, and technical controls that make the safe path easier than shadow work.

  • Security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS said AI-assisted code creation is increasing code sprawl as employees build applications, scripts, automations, and agents outside approved security review processes.
  • A RedAccess report cited in the article found 380,000 publicly accessible assets on vibe coding platforms including Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, with roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information.
  • Datadog’s Matt Muller said banning AI tools can push employees into less visible shadow channels, so Datadog aims to provide approved AI tools through a centralized internal marketplace.
  • ASOS’s Indu Sajeev said governance cannot rely only on paper policies and described using a registry to track AI use cases, associated human identities, and accountability.
  • Jamf’s Mario Villatoro said preventing unmanaged code sprawl depends on enabling employees with the right tools, training, acceptable use policies, and correctly categorized data.

The Invisible Problem: Why OT Cyber Risk Is Hard to Prioritise — Optional

OT cyber risk is hard to prioritise because vulnerabilities can remain invisible while operations continue normally, making fixes seem riskier than inaction. The article argues that OT teams need clearer, context-specific risk information tied to operational impact rather than more alerts or technical severity lists.

  • OT cyber risk is difficult to prioritise because a vulnerable device can continue operating normally without visible signs of failure.
  • Operational leaders must balance cyber security against production schedules and the risk that a poorly executed fix could create a bigger problem.
  • The article argues that more dashboards, alerts, and lengthy technical reports can make OT cyber security harder rather than easier for operational teams.
  • Plant managers need to know which vulnerabilities matter in their specific environment and which could affect production, safety, or quality.
  • The article says useful OT cyber security work translates technical exposure into operational impact through continuous assessment, passive monitoring, and clear language.

The Onboarding Password Mistake That Creates Unnecessary Risk — Optional

The article warns that sharing temporary onboarding passwords by email, SMS, or phone creates avoidable risk because those credentials can be intercepted, mishandled, or left unchanged. It promotes letting new employees securely create their own first-day passwords to reduce exposure and simplify onboarding.

  • Organizations often send temporary first-day passwords to new employees by email, SMS, or phone, which can expose credentials if messages are intercepted, forwarded, accessed on unsecured devices, or relayed by third parties.
  • The article says temporary onboarding passwords can become long-term weaknesses when users do not change them, workflows fail to enforce resets, or the credentials remain active unnoticed.
  • Specops First Day Password, available as part of Specops uReset, lets new employees set their own password through a secure enrollment process instead of receiving a temporary credential.
  • In November 2023, Cyber Av3ngers exploited Unitronics PLCs using the default credential “1111” at the Municipal Water Authority of Aliquippa in Pennsylvania.
  • In 2025, researchers found that McDonald’s McHire platform could be accessed through a weak legacy administrator account reportedly using “123456” as both the username and password.

⚡ Weekly Recap: Chrome 0-Day, UniFi Exploits, macOS Stealers, VPN Flaw and More — Optional

Attackers are actively exploiting fresh and neglected weaknesses across widely used platforms, including Chrome, Oracle PeopleSoft, Check Point VPN, UniFi OS, and abandoned Arch Linux AUR packages. The key takeaway is to patch urgent CVEs quickly and audit deprecated, exposed, or forgotten software before it becomes an entry point.

  • Google released Chrome security updates for 74 vulnerabilities, including the actively exploited V8 zero-day CVE-2026-11645.
  • ShinyHunters exploited an unpatched Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day, CVE-2026-35273, in attacks that mainly targeted the higher education sector.
  • Unknown threat actors compromised more than 1,500 abandoned Arch User Repository packages with malicious preinstall scripts that downloaded and executed the atomic-lockfile npm package.
  • Check Point warned that CVE-2026-50751 is being actively exploited against Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 protocol.
  • Microsoft warned that threat actors are using AI-themed lures in phishing, malvertising, and SEO-driven campaigns that lead to credential theft, financial fraud, or malware infection.

15th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

Check Point’s 15 June threat intelligence report highlights a busy week of major breaches, active zero-day exploitation, AI-related security risks, and critical patches, including ShinyHunters’ Oracle PeopleSoft attacks, a Check Point VPN authentication bypass tied to Qilin ransomware, and Microsoft’s record Patch Tuesday. Organizations should prioritize patching exposed enterprise systems and reviewing AI, supply-chain, and remote-access risks.

  • ShinyHunters breached the University of Nottingham’s student records system, affecting about 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records.
  • Check Point Research identified active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments using the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol.
  • Microsoft released its largest Patch Tuesday update to date, addressing more than 200 Windows and Defender vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-45657, CVE-2026-41091, and CVE-2026-50507.
  • Check Point Research demonstrated exploitable flaws in LangGraph by chaining SQL injection and unsafe deserialization issues to achieve remote code execution.
  • Researchers detected a supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware.

Webinar: How behavioral AI stops phishing and account takeovers — Optional

BleepingComputer is hosting a July 8, 2026 webinar on how behavioral AI can help detect and automate response to phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks that bypass traditional email security and MFA controls.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will examine how phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • The article says attackers increasingly abuse legitimate authentication flows and compromised accounts to evade detection and maintain persistent access.
  • The webinar will discuss how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows across inbound email threats and compromised accounts.
  • Attendees will learn approaches for reducing manual investigation work, improving detection accuracy, and building a faster automated approach to email security.

item.title: FBI Disrupts Outsider Enterprise AI-Powered Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
item.description: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing sites, more than a million fraudulent URLs, SMS delivery at scale, and AI-enabled tooling. The case reflects continued convergence between cybercrime infrastructure, mobile messaging abuse, automation, and hosted e-commerce-style service models. It also highlights growing collaboration between law enforcement, cloud and internet infrastructure providers, telecommunications carriers, and security researchers in disrupting fraud operations before messages and phishing links reach end users. The FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, dismantled infrastructure tied to Outsider Enterprise, a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation accused of running thousands of fraudulent websites designed to steal credit card data and passwords. Google linked the operation to approximately 9,000 fake websites and more than one million fraudulent URLs, illustrating the scale at which the group generated and distributed phishing infrastructure. Authorities believe campaigns connected to Outsider Enterprise stole more than 3.8 million credit card records and caused an estimated $1.9 billion in losses. During the disruption, the FBI and partners seized multiple administration servers, a Shopify storefront, a testing account, roughly $100,000 in USDT, and a Telegram bot associated with the operation. The seized assets point to a mature service model spanning web hosting, testing workflows, payment infrastructure, and operator communications. Google also filed a civil lawsuit targeting the operation’s infrastructure and said it is coordinating with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block fraudulent messages before they reach subscribers. According to Google, Outsider Enterprise sent 2.5 million SMS messages to Android users over a two-week period in May, and Android users flagged 55,000 of those messages as fraudulent. The reported SMS volume underscores the role of mobile delivery channels in modern phishing operations and the operational emphasis on rapid, high-volume lure distribution. The reporting characterizes Outsider Enterprise as AI-powered, reflecting the broader trend of fraud services adopting automation and generative tooling to scale phishing content, infrastructure, and campaign management. While the article focuses on the takedown rather than technical indicators, the scope of domains, URLs, SMS traffic, and stolen payment records makes the operation one of the more significant recent examples of phishing-as-a-service disruption.

item.title: InformData-Impersonation Phishing Passed DMARC via Authorized SendGrid Infrastructure
item.description: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email infrastructure can allow malicious messages to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks even under strict enforcement. IRONSCALES reported an employment verification-themed phishing email impersonating InformData that was sent through SendGrid infrastructure and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, including a DMARC policy set to REJECT. The message was associated with SendGrid host wfbtrqkw.outbound-mail.sendgrid.net and IP address 159[.]183[.]84[.]25, which the article states were configured as authorized sending infrastructure for international.informdata[.]com. The phishing lure targeted a sports data technology company and was flagged in June 2026 after a colleague at the organization reported the email. According to IRONSCALES, one mailbox was quarantined. The message used an employment verification pretext while redirecting replies and user interaction away from the apparent sender’s legitimate domain. The email’s Reply-To header directed responses to support@verifying[.]you, while the call-to-action linked to verify.zippedscript[.]com; both were described by IRONSCALES as attacker-controlled domains. This combination created a mismatch between the authenticated sending domain and the destinations used for follow-up interaction, allowing the message to appear technically authenticated while steering the recipient into attacker-controlled communication paths. The case illustrates the distinction between domain authentication and message intent. As described in the report, DMARC validated that the message was sent via infrastructure authorized for the domain, but it did not determine whether the email content, Reply-To destination, or embedded link represented a legitimate business process. For defenders and threat hunters, the incident is notable because the malicious indicators were not authentication failures, but rather the use of authorized infrastructure alongside suspicious reply routing and external verification domains.
